summary:
The Dong Son Culture (c. 500–100 BC) was a Bronze and Iron Age culture centered in the Red River Delta of modern Vietnam, best known for its spectacular bronze drums — massive ceremonial objects decorated with intricate geometric patterns and figural scenes — which have been found across Southeast Asia and represent one of the region's most distinctive early artistic traditions.

Dong Son (named for the village in Thanh Hoa province where the culture was first identified) was a sophisticated society that combined rice agriculture, fishing, and metalworking. The Dong Son bronze drums — some weighing hundreds of kilograms — were prestige objects used in ceremonies, redistributed through trade networks across mainland and island Southeast Asia, and buried with elites as markers of status. The drums' decorations show scenes of ritual dances, warriors, boats, and birds that illuminate early Vietnamese religious life. The Dong Son culture corresponds roughly with the period of the Au Lac kingdom and the legendary Hong Bang dynasty of Vietnamese historical tradition. Chinese [[event:1134|Nanyue Kingdom]] influence and eventual Han conquest ultimately absorbed the Dong Son cultural sphere into the Chinese world, though distinctive Vietnamese cultural elements persisted.
